content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 197–198 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_002_page_001.jpg, DEU_002_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 144–145

---

1 Then we turned and journeyed into the wilderness by the way of the Reed Sea, as the LORD spoke to me, and we went around Mount Se'ir many days.

2 And the LORD said to me, saying:

3 "You have gone around this mountain long enough; turn northward.

4 And command the people, saying: 'You are passing through the border of your brothers the sons of Esav, who dwell in Se'ir, and they will fear you — so you shall watch yourselves very carefully.

5 Do not provoke them, for I will not give you from their land even a foot's tread, because I have given Mount Se'ir to Esav as a possession.

6 You shall buy food from them with silver, that you may eat; and also water you shall purchase from them with silver, that you may drink.

7 For the LORD your God has blessed you in all the work of your hand. He has known your walking through this great wilderness; these forty years the LORD your God [has been] with you — you have lacked nothing.'"

8 And we passed on from our brothers the sons of Esav who dwelt in Se'ir, from the way of the Arabah, from Eilat and from Etsyon-gever. And we turned and passed by the way of the wilderness of Mo'av.

9 And the LORD said to me: "Do not harass Mo'av and do not provoke war against them, for I will not give you from their land a possession, because to the sons of Lot I have given Ar as a possession.

10 The Emim formerly dwelt there — a people great and numerous and tall as the Anakim.

11 They also are reckoned as Refa'im, like the Anakim, but the Mo'avites call them Emim.

12 And in Se'ir the Chorim formerly dwelt, but the sons of Esav dispossessed them and destroyed them from before them and settled in their place — just as Yisra'el did to the land of their possession which the LORD gave to them.

13 Now arise and cross over the Wadi Zered." And we crossed over the Wadi Zered.

14 And the days that we walked from Kadesh-barnea until we crossed the Wadi Zered [were] thirty-eight years, until the entire generation of the men of war had perished from the midst of the camp, as the LORD swore to them.

15 And also the hand of the LORD was against them, to destroy them from the midst of the camp, until their perishing.

16 And it was, when all the men of war had finished dying from the midst of the people,

17 that the LORD spoke to me, saying:

18 "You are passing over today the border of Mo'av, [past] Ar.

19 And you will approach opposite the sons of Ammon; do not harass them and do not provoke against them, for I will not give from the land of the sons of Ammon to you as a possession, because to the sons of Lot I have given it as a possession."

20 (That also is reckoned as a land of Refa'im; Refa'im formerly dwelt there, and the Ammonites call them Zamzummim —

21 a people great and numerous and tall as the Anakim; and the LORD destroyed them from before them, and they dispossessed them and settled in their place —

22 as he did for the sons of Esav who dwell in Se'ir, in that he destroyed the Chorim from before them, and they dispossessed them and settled in their place until this day.

23 And the Avvim who dwelt in villages as far as Gaza — the Kaphtorim who came out from Kaphtor destroyed them and settled in their place.)

24 "Arise, journey and cross the Wadi Arnon. See — I have given into your hand Sichon the Emorite king of Heshbon, and his land. Begin to possess [it], and provoke war against him.

25 This day I will begin to put the dread of you and the fear of you upon the faces of the peoples under all the heavens, who shall hear the report of you and shall tremble and writhe because of you."

26 And I sent messengers from the wilderness of Kedemot to Sichon king of Heshbon with words of peace, saying:

27 "Let me pass through your land; on the road I will go, I will not turn aside to the right or to the left.

28 Food you shall sell me for silver, that I may eat, and water you shall give me for silver, that I may drink; only let me pass through on foot —

29 as the sons of Esav who dwell in Se'ir did for me and the Mo'avites who dwell in Ar — until I cross the Yarden to the land that the LORD our God gives to us."

30 But Sichon king of Heshbon was not willing to let us pass through him, for the LORD your God hardened his spirit and made his heart stubborn, in order to give him into your hand, as [it is] this day.

31 And the LORD said to me: "See — I have begun to give before you Sichon and his land. Begin, take possession, to possess his land."

32 And Sichon came out to meet us — he and all his people — to battle at Yahats.

33 And the LORD our God gave him before us, and we struck him and his sons and all his people.

34 And we captured all his cities at that time and devoted to destruction every city — men and women and little ones; we left no survivor.

35 Only the livestock we plundered for ourselves, and the spoil of the cities that we had captured.

36 From Aro'er, which is on the edge of the Wadi Arnon, and from the city that is in the wadi, even to Gil'ad, there was not a city too strong for us; the LORD our God gave all before us.

37 Only to the land of the sons of Ammon you did not draw near — all the bank of the Wadi Yabbok, and the cities of the hill-country, and all that the LORD our God commanded us.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**v.1 — by the way of the Reed Sea:** *derekh Yam Suph* — the Gulf of Aqaba arm of the Red Sea, here specifically the eastern branch. This is not the sea of the Exodus crossing but the southern detour around Edomite territory.

**v.3 — turn northward:** *penu lakhem tsaphonah* — literally "turn for yourselves northward." The reflexive *lakhem* is a dativus ethicus; EMET elides it. The LXX (p. 144): *ἐπιστράφητε οὖν ὑμεῖς βορρᾶν* — adds "therefore" (*οὖν*) not in MT.

**v.4 — the sons of Esav who dwell in Se'ir:** Esav/Edom occupies Se'ir as a divine grant parallel to Yisra'el's inheritance of Canaan. The text demonstrates a pattern: displaced indigenous peoples, then new occupants by divine assignment. This parenthetical anthropological note (vv. 10–12, 20–23) is characteristic of Deuteronomy's "historical footnotes."

**v.5 — foot's tread:** *ad midrak kaf-ragel* — literally "as far as a tread of the sole of a foot." An idiom for the smallest possible parcel of land. The LXX: *βῆμα ποδός* (a step of a foot) — idiomatic Greek equivalent.

**v.7 — He has known your walking:** *yada' lekhtekha* — the verb *yada'* (to know) here carries the sense of intimate providential attention. EMET renders it straightforwardly; modern translations often soften to "He has watched over your journey."

**v.9 — Ar:** A city or region of Mo'av, here used synecdochically for the whole Moabite territory. The LXX: *τὴν Αρ* — treated as a proper noun.

**vv.10–12, 20–23 — The anthropological parentheses:** These parenthetical notes are among the most remarkable features of Deuteronomy 2. They describe pre-Israelite giant peoples: Emim (Mo'avite name for Refa'im), Zamzummim (Ammonite name for Refa'im), Chorim (dispossessed by Esav's descendants), and Avvim (dispossessed by Kaphtorim/Philistines from Crete). The pattern argues from analogy: if God gave other nations their lands by displacing earlier inhabitants, so too with Yisra'el. The LXX (p. 144–145) preserves all these parentheses. Note that the Vaticanus on p. 144 shows these sections clearly marked in the scriptio continua.

**v.14 — thirty-eight years:** *shloshim u-shmoneh shanah* — from Kadesh-barnea to crossing the Wadi Zered. The total wilderness period was forty years (1:3), minus the two years from Egypt to Kadesh. This is the only explicit statement of the duration of the punishment period in the Pentateuch.

**v.20 — Zamzummim:** The LXX (p. 145) renders this as *Ζομζομμιν* — a transliteration. Some manuscripts have *Ζαμζουμμίν*. The MT here has *Zamzummim*, which some scholars connect to an onomatopoeia for "murmurers" or relate to the root *zmzm*.

**v.23 — Kaphtorim:** Identified in scholarly tradition with Crete or the Aegean coast. They displaced the Avvim from the Gazan coastal strip — a historical datum that places Philistine prehistory within the Deuteronomic theological framework.

**v.25 — they shall tremble and writhe:** *ve-chalu ve-chagu* — two verbs expressing physical distress: *chul* (to writhe, tremble in birth pangs) and *chag* is likely *chagag* or a variant — EMET reads the MT as *yir'gu ve-chalu*, "they shall fear and writhe." The LXX (p. 145): *ταραχθήσονται καὶ ὠδινήσουσιν* — troubled and shall suffer birth pangs — supporting the MT.

**v.30 — hardened his spirit and made his heart stubborn:** *hikshah et-rucho ve-imets et-levavo* — two verbs, two nouns: *ruach* (spirit/breath) made hard (*kasheh*), and *lev* (heart) made firm (*amets*). The divine hardening pattern echoes the Pharaoh narrative. The LXX: *ἐσκλήρυνεν Κύριος ὁ θεός σου τὸ πνεῦμα αὐτοῦ καὶ κατίσχυσεν τὴν καρδίαν αὐτοῦ* — exact correspondence to the two-part Hebrew structure.

**v.34 — devoted to destruction:** *va-nachtarem* — Hiphil of *charam*, the term for sacred destruction (cherem). Every person was placed under the ban; nothing was spared except livestock (v.35) and inanimate spoil. This is the first explicit application of the cherem in Deuteronomy.

**v.36 — Aro'er:** *Aro'er asher al-sefat nachal Arnon* — "Aro'er which is on the lip of the Wadi Arnon." The word *sefat* (lip, edge) is used for riverbanks throughout the OT. The LXX: *ἐπὶ χείλους χειμάρρου Αρνων* — "on the lip of the torrent of Arnon."

— EMET
